questions.text: When I learn that someone I know is going through a difficult time, my first instinct is to consider how I might help.
I can listen to someone describe their suffering and maintain complete emotional equilibrium.
I find that other people's pain lingers in my mind long after the conversation ends.
In a crisis, I become more effective when I set aside emotional reactions and focus on what needs to be done.
I feel a genuine sense of responsibility when I have the means to reduce someone's suffering.
I process other people's hardships primarily as problems to be solved rather than emotions to be shared.
When someone shares a painful experience, the fact of their pain carries real weight in my mind.
I believe that maintaining emotional distance from a problem allows me to offer more useful help.
The knowledge that someone is in pain, even someone I have never met, creates a pull in me to respond.
I can assess a suffering person's situation clearly precisely because I am not emotionally affected by it.
I feel a deep sense of responsibility for the safety and wellbeing of those around me.
I have noticed that I respond to others' emotional pain with analysis rather than with feeling.
When I help someone, the motivation comes from a deep sense that their wellbeing matters to me personally.
I tend to view others' distress through a lens of objective evaluation rather than personal resonance.
I believe that truly understanding another's suffering requires recognizing it as a priority that demands a response.
I think of emotional reactions to others' pain as something that can be managed and modulated at will.
I feel that the weight of another person's struggle is something I carry with me, not something I observe from a distance.
I am able to make difficult decisions that affect others without my emotions interfering with my judgment.
